source_metadata.title_raw: Jeffrey A. Walter - A-19-130 - June 24, 2019 - Sonoma

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Jeffrey A. Walter 
City Attorney of Sonoma 
670 W. Napa St, Suite F 
Sonoma, CA 95476 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-130 
Dear Mr. Walter: 
June 24, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
· 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
May the Vice Mayor of Sonoma take part in the City Council's decision to adopt or reject a 
proposed ordinance that would permit a small number of cannabis-related businesses to operate in 
the City's commercial and mixed use zones, given that the Vice Mayor rents an apartment near 
properties within those zones? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes, because any financial effect the ordinance may have on the Vice Mayor's leasehold 
interest is too theoretical, it is not reasonable foreseeable the decision will have a material effect on 
the Vice Mayor's interest. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
File No. A-19.-130 
Page No. 2 
Sonoma ("City") City Councilmember and Vice Mayor, Logan Harvey, lives in an 
apartment which he rents from the owners of Duggan's Funeral Home in Sonoma, California. His 
lease agreement has a term of one year, after which it will convert to a month-to-month tenancy. 
The rental rate is adjusted each year by the landlord. To Vice Mayor Harvey's belief, the Duggan 
family that operates the funeral home beneath his apartment intends to continue operating the 
funeral home for the indefinite future. 
Except for deliveries of medicinal cannabis and products from licensed businesses located 
outside of the City's boundaries, all other adult-use and medicinal commercial cannabis activities 
are currently prohibited in all zones throughout the City. The City Council will soon consider the 
adoption of a proposed ordinance amending the City's zoning code (referred to as the "development 
code" in Sonoma) to permit the establishment and operation of no more than four specified 
commercial cannabis businesses, provided that those businesses first obtain, among other things, a 
use permit. The proposed ordinance would allow a single store-front retail dispensary to be located 
only in the City's Commercial (C) zoning district. A non-store-front retail dispensary, a testing 
laboratory, and a non-volatile manufacturing business could conditionally be permitted .in the C 
zoning district or the Mixed Use (MX) zoning district. Significantly, the proposed ordinance 
identifies four areas designated as "buffer" areas (typically surrounding schools, day care centers, 
and the historic town plaza) in which no commercial cannabis activities would be permitted 
irrespective of whether that buffer area includes C- and/or MX-zoned parcels. 
Adoption of the proposed ordinance does not approve the establishment of any commercial 
cannabis business. Rather, it specifies where they may and may not be located. There are 408 
parcels in the MX zone and 311 parcels in the C zone, any of which could be the site of a 
commercial cannabis business (except that the single store-front retail business would be required to 
be located in the C zone). Vice Mayor Harvey's apartment is within 500 feet of some C- and MX-
_zoned properties, i.e., potential sites of cannabis-related businesses. 
ANALYSIS 
Under Section 87100, "[ n ]o public official at any level of state or local government shall 
make, participate in making or in any way attempt to use his official position to influence a 
governmental decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a financial interest." 
Section 87103 provides, "[a] public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning 
of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial 
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her 
immediate family," or on certain enumerated economic interests, including "[a]ny real property in 
which the public official has a direct or indirect interest worth two thousand dollars ($2,000) or 
more." 
A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if 
the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or 
theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).) If the financial effect cannot be 
expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's control, it is not 
reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid) 


FileNo. A-19-130 
__ Page No. 3 
The reasonably foreseeable financial effects of a governmental decision on any real property 
in which a governmental official has a leasehold interest as the lessee of the property is material 
only if the governmental decision will: 
(1) 
Change the termination date of the lease; 
(2) 
Increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; 
(3) 
Change the official's actual or legally allowable use of the property; or 
(4) 
Impact the official's use and enjoyment of the property. 
The proposed ordinance would permit a total of four cannabis-related business locations 
within the City's C and MX zoning locations, and only one of those businesses would be a store-
front retail dispensary. The ordinance would not select any specific locations within the 700-plus 
potential sites in the C- and MX-zones. Rather, the ordinance would merely permit a new industry 
to begin operating amongst the other industries that already make up the C- and MX-zones. At this 
point, any financial effect the ordinance may ultimately have on Vice Mayor Harvey's leasehold 
interest is entirely hypothetical. Though it is possible that a cannabis-related business could open 
near Vice Mayor Harvey's apartment, that is a subsequent question separate from whether to allow 
a small number of cannabis-related businesses to open somewhere in the City's C- and MX-zones. 
Additionally, the City Council's decision would not change the termination date of Vice 
Mayor Harvey's lease. There is also nothing to indicate that the introduction of a limited number of 
cannabis-related businesses would increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property or 
affect Vice Mayor Harvey's use and enjoyment of the property, particularly given that numerous . 
other businesses are already permitted to operate within those zones and, in fact, Harvey's 
apartment is above one business. Thus, it is not reasonably foreseeable the decisions regarding the 
ordinance will have a material effect on the Vice Mayor Harvey's leasehold interest is not 
prohibited from taking part in the City Council's decision to adopt or reject the ordinance. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
KMC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
Kevin Cornwall 
Counsel, Legal Division

properties within those zones?

QUESTION: May the Vice Mayor of Sonoma take part in the City Council's decision to adopt or reject a 
proposed ordinance that would permit a small number of cannabis-related businesses to operate in 
the City's commercial and mixed use zones, given that the Vice Mayor rents an apartment near 
properties within those zones?

CONCLUSION: Yes, because any financial effect the ordinance may have on the Vice Mayor's leasehold 
interest is too theoretical, it is not reasonable foreseeable the decision will have a material effect on 
the Vice Mayor's interest.
